subcategory: Clumping timber and edible shoot bamboo

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 7–11; Giant Timber Bamboo (Oldham Bamboo) is the most widely planted clumping bamboo in the Southern United States and California — a large, non-invasive clumper native to Taiwan and south China producing straight, thick-walled culms to 65 feet tall and 4–5 inches diameter, used for timber, stakes, furniture, and edible young shoots.

directions: Plant in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained soil; Bambusa oldhamii is a clumping bamboo that does not run — the rhizomes are pachymorph (tightly clumping), so it stays where you plant it and never invades neighbors; water deeply and regularly in establishment and during summer heat. Bambusa oldhamii (Giant Timber Bamboo, Oldham Bamboo, or Eating Bamboo) is a large, fast-growing, non-invasive clumping bamboo native to Taiwan and adjacent southeastern China, and the most widely planted timber bamboo in California, Florida, and the Gulf Coast states due to its massive size, non-running growth habit, and cold hardiness to zone 7b. The culms (canes) grow rapidly to 50–65 feet tall and 3–5 inches in diameter, straight-walled and deep green when young, maturing to yellow-green or golden. The shoots produced in spring are among the largest, most palatable, and best-tasting of any bamboo available in the United States — harvested when 6–12 inches tall, boiled to remove bitterness, and used in Asian cooking exactly like commercial canned bamboo shoots. Giant Timber Bamboo also produces outstanding landscape screening, windbreak, and carbon-sequestration value — fully grown culms can be harvested every 3–5 years as timber for furniture, flooring, fencing, and structural poles. Excellent Gulf Coast and zone 8–11 privacy screen, grove specimen, and food-producing landscape plant.
sources: University of Florida IFAS — Bamboo for Florida Landscapes

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring with STRONG root barrier (minimum 30-inch deep HDPE rhizome barrier buried 28–30 inches deep) in full sun in fertile, moist, well-drained soil in zone 5–10; Madake is the premier Japanese giant timber bamboo and one of the largest of all Phyllostachys species — a running bamboo that WILL spread aggressively without containment — producing straight, thick-walled culms to 75 feet tall used for traditional Japanese flutes, tool handles, and as the finest furniture bamboo.

directions: Plant with strong, deep rhizome barrier (30-inch HDPE sheet) to prevent running — Phyllostachys bambusoides sends rhizomes 20–30+ feet in a season and will colonize large areas without containment; install barrier before planting and inspect annually for rhizome escape. Phyllostachys bambusoides (Madake, Japanese Giant Timber Bamboo, or Matake) is one of the largest and most culturally important bamboos in the world — native to China but cultivated in Japan for at least 1,500 years, where it is the premier timber bamboo of Japanese traditional crafts. The culms grow to extraordinary dimensions in optimal conditions: 50–75 feet tall and 4–6 inches in diameter, with thick, straight, dense walls, outstanding tensile strength, and beautiful, lustrous, deep-green color that ages to golden-yellow. Madake is the traditional material for shakuhachi (Japanese end-blown flutes), kendo practice swords (shinai), fishing rods, scaffolding, architectural elements, and fine bamboo furniture — the culm quality is unsurpassed among temperate bamboos. The young spring shoots are edible. As a Phyllostachys (running bamboo), however, it produces aggressive, far-traveling monopodial rhizomes that can penetrate asphalt, damage foundations, and invade neighbor properties if not properly contained by deep rhizome barriers. Never plant without barriers in place. Extremely cold hardy to zone 5 when established.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in partial shade to full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 5–9; Umbrella Bamboo is the ideal bamboo for most temperate gardens — a non-invasive, extremely cold-hardy clumping bamboo native to the mountains of Hunan Province, China, forming elegant, arching, fountain-like clumps of slender culms and fine-textured leaves — completely non-invasive and perfect for small to medium gardens needing screening or specimen interest without rhizome spreading.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, fertile soil; Fargesia murielae performs best in cool, moist conditions with some shade protection from hot afternoon sun — in hot climates (zone 7b–9) choose a north-facing or east-facing position to prevent leaf scorching in summer heat. Fargesia murielae (Umbrella Bamboo or Muriel Bamboo) is one of the most popular and widely grown bamboos for temperate gardens across Europe and North America, prized precisely for its qualities opposite to the notorious running bamboos: it is a tight, non-invasive clumper with pachymorph rhizomes that never run or spread aggressively. Native to the high mountains of Hunan and Sichuan Provinces, China, at elevations of 6,000–10,000 feet, it is extremely cold hardy to zone 5 (-10°F) — surviving conditions that kill most bamboos. The graceful, arching culms (1–2 inch diameter, 6–15 feet tall) are covered with fine-textured, lance-shaped leaves that cascade in elegant, weeping fountain-form clumps — outstanding as a specimen plant, privacy screen, or container plant. Fargesia murielae is the primary food bamboo of the wild giant panda in Hunan Province — nearly all wild Fargesia murielae populations in China flowered and died simultaneously in the 1980s in a masting event, causing a severe panda food shortage. Important note: all Fargesia species are monocarpic — they flower once after 80–100+ years and die; plants from the post-1980s regeneration are not yet at flowering age.
sources: WWF Giant Panda — Bamboo Food Plants

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in partial to deep shade in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in zone 5–8; Fountain Bamboo is the most shade-tolerant clumping bamboo available for temperate gardens — a cold-hardy, non-invasive mountain bamboo native to Sichuan and Yunnan Province, China, producing slender, gracefully arching, deep-green to purple-tinted culms and fine-textured foliage in elegant fountain-like clumps ideal for woodland and shade garden settings.

directions: Plant in partial to deep shade with consistent moisture and protection from hot afternoon sun — Fargesia nitida is the premier shade-tolerant bamboo for woodland and north-facing garden positions; mulch heavily to maintain cool, moist root conditions. Fargesia nitida (Fountain Bamboo, Blue Fountain Bamboo, or Chinese Fountain Bamboo) is an elegant, slow-growing, non-invasive clumping bamboo native to the high montane forests and alpine meadow edges of Sichuan, Yunnan, and adjacent western Chinese mountains at elevations of 8,000–12,000 feet, where it is a primary food bamboo of the giant panda alongside F. murielae. In garden cultivation, F. nitida is recognized as the most shade-tolerant of all hardy bamboos available — thriving in deep shade positions where other bamboos refuse to grow — and is particularly valuable for woodland garden settings, shaded north-facing boundaries, and forest understory plantings. The slender culms (0.5–1 inch diameter, 4–12 feet tall) often develop attractive purple, red, or dark-tinted coloration in the nodes and internodes — particularly on new culms — contrasting beautifully with the fine, dark-green leaves. Like F. murielae, it is a monocarpic species (flowers once after decades and dies), and the current garden plants have 60–80+ years of growth before the next masting. Cold-hardy to zone 5; prefers cool summers and suffers in prolonged heat above 90°F.

planting.note: Plant divisions or container stock in spring in full sun in moist, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in zone 9–12; Guadua Bamboo is the premier structural and construction bamboo of the Americas — a large, clumping, thorny bamboo native to the Andean foothills and tropical valleys of Colombia, Ecuador, and Venezuela, producing culms to 100 feet tall and 6 inches diameter with extraordinary tensile strength — used as the primary structural material for earthquake-resistant bamboo construction throughout Colombia and Ecuador.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun; Guadua angustifolia requires abundant water and high fertility to achieve maximum growth — water deeply and regularly, apply organic mulch, and fertilize generously with nitrogen for maximum culm production. Guadua angustifolia (Guadua Bamboo, Colombian Bamboo, or American Bamboo) is a giant, clumping, thorny bamboo native to the tropical Andean foothills, river valleys, and lowland forests of Colombia, Ecuador, Venezuela, and Bolivia, and one of the most structurally important plants in the Americas. The culms reach exceptional dimensions — 80–100+ feet tall and 4–6 inches in diameter — with extraordinarily thick, dense walls and one of the highest tensile strengths of any plant material, comparable to mild steel on a weight-for-weight basis. Guadua is the traditional structural material for vernacular architecture across the Colombian and Ecuadorian Andean region and has been formally recognized by international structural engineering standards for earthquake-resistant construction — notably, several Colombian cities survived devastating earthquakes in the 1990s with significantly less loss of life in guadua-framed structures than in concrete buildings. The Nobel Prize-winning architect Simón Vélez has pioneered modern guadua structural architecture internationally. Guadua also provides exceptional carbon sequestration, watershed stabilization, and riparian buffer value in tropical environments where it occurs naturally.
sources: INBAR International Bamboo and Rattan — Guadua angustifolia
Colombia National Bamboo Program — Guadua angustifolia

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in tropical or subtropical climates (zone 10–12) in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in partial shade with tall trees or structures to climb; Rattan (Calamus rotang) is a clustering, spiny, climbing palm native to the tropical forests of India and Sri Lanka — the original source of 'rattan' furniture cane — producing extraordinarily long, flexible, spiny stems that climb forest canopy trees using backward-pointing hook-tipped whips.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in partial shade with tall trees or very strong support structures for climbing — Calamus rotang is a vigorous, long-stemmed climber that can reach enormous heights in mature forest and requires substantial support to reach fruiting size; handle with thick gloves as the entire plant is armed with sharp, recurved spines and hooks. Calamus rotang (Rattan, Common Rattan, or Indian Rattan Palm) is a clustering, spiny, climbing palm in the Arecaceae family native to the tropical coastal forests, moist forests, and forest edges of southern India (Kerala, Tamil Nadu) and Sri Lanka, and widely naturalized throughout Southeast Asia — the progenitor of the global rattan furniture and basketry industry. Calamus rotang and its relatives (Calamus species are the largest genus in the palm family, with 300+ species) produce extraordinarily long, slender, solid, flexible, spiny stems by climbing to the forest canopy using backward-pointing, hook-covered whips (cirri) that grab onto adjacent vegetation. The harvested and processed stems — stripped of spines, bundled, and steamed — are the 'rattan cane' used worldwide for wicker furniture, baskets, mats, walking sticks, and traditional crafts. Global rattan is a multi-billion-dollar industry, primarily from Indonesian and Malaysian forests. Calamus rotang is one of the few rattan species adapted to cooler, drier conditions outside equatorial rainforest and can be grown as an ornamental curiosity in zone 10–12 gardens with ample space and support.
